coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots-linked isophorone diisocyanate and β-cyclodextrin for detection of chromium ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jie
Qiu, Fengxian
Wu, Haiyan
Li, Xin
Zhang, Tao
Niu, Xiangheng
Yang, Dongya
Pan, Jiangming
Xu, Jicheng
coredata.dc:description: A water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) from cellulose was prepared using one-pot simple hydrothermal method. In this work, a novel fluorescent probe material, fluorescent carbon dots-linked isophorone diisocyanate and β-cyclodextrin (FCDs-IPDI-CD), was prepared with FCDs, isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as raw materials. The structure and morphology of FCDs-IPDI-CD were characterized using the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The as-prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD exhibits excellent emission property and high stability. The fluorescence of the FCDs-IPDI-CD could be quenched by Cr(VI) ions, and the results indicate that FCDs-IPDI-CD can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI) ions with good selectivity and sensitivity in an aqueous solution. The influences of environment factors (such as pH, reaction time) on relative fluorescence intensity were studied. According to the optimum conditions, a new sensitive method detecting Cr(VI) ions was established. The method has been successfully applied to detect Cr(VI) ions in water and soil samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Isophorone diisocyanate
β-Cyclodextrin
Cr(VI) ions
Quenching

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of Prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD for Detection of Cr(VI) in Soil
The Influences of the Different Hydrothermal Temperature and Time on the Fluorescent Spectra of FCDs
The Effects of Some Factors on the Fluorescent Intensity of the as-Prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD Solution
The Interaction Between the Fluorescent FCDs-IPDI-CD and Cr(VI) Ions
Optimization of the Conditions for Determining Cr(VI)
The Selectivity of FCDs-IPDI-CD Toward Cr(VI) Ions
Application of Prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD for Detection of Cr(VI) in Water and Soil Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The influences of the different hydrothermal temperature (a) and hydrothermal time (b) on the fluorescent spectra of CDs; UV–vis absorption (c) and fluorescence spectra (d) of FCDs-IPDI-CD.
TEM image of FCDs-IPDI-CD (a) and FCDs (b); and FT-IR spectra (c) of CDs, β-CD and FCDs-IPDI-CD.
Effects of FCDs-IPDI-CD concentration (a), pH (b) of solution and preservation time (c) on the fluorescent spectra of FCDs-IPDI-CD.
Fluorescence spectra of FCDs-IPDI-CD in the absence and in the presence of metal ions.
Effect of different concentration of Cr(VI) ions (a) on fluorescence spectra; the standard linear curve of the reaction system (b).
Effects of different pH value (a) and reaction time (b) on relative fluorescence intensity.
Selectivity of Cr(VI) using FCDs-IPDI-CD as probes.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots-linked isophorone diisocyanate and β-cyclodextrin for detection of chromium ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Zhenjiang College, Zhenjiang 212003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) from cellulose was prepared using one-pot simple hydrothermal method. In this work, a novel fluorescent probe material, fluorescent carbon dots-linked isophorone diisocyanate and β-cyclodextrin (FCDs-IPDI-CD), was prepared with FCDs, isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as raw materials. The structure and morphology of FCDs-IPDI-CD were characterized using the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The as-prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD exhibits excellent emission property and high stability. The fluorescence of the FCDs-IPDI-CD could be quenched by Cr(VI) ions, and the results indicate that FCDs-IPDI-CD can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI) ions with good selectivity and sensitivity in an aqueous solution. The influences of environment factors (such as pH, reaction time) on relative fluorescence intensity were studied. According to the optimum conditions, a new sensitive method detecting Cr(VI) ions was established. The method has been successfully applied to detect Cr(VI) ions in water and soil samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fluorescent nanoparticles have very attracted attentions owing to their promising applications in many fields such as biosensing, biology labeling, optoelectronic devices and so on [1–4]. Carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanotubes (CNT), fluorescent graphene, nanodiamonds and semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) belong to this kind of material. Quantum dots have attracted interests of multitudinous scientists during the recent decades [5–7]. Among these nanoparticles, SQDs usually have some properties such as size-tunable, narrow emission spectra and high photo stability. In addition, SQDs also have resistance to metabolic degradation in bioapplications. However, people have recognized that the well-developed QDs are limited by toxic heavy metal ions such as cadmium ions and lead ions, which occur in the application process [8–10]. As a result, searching for characteristic and practical fluorescent nanopartilces that can substitute for QDs has turned into current development trends. A prominent representative is carbon dots [11,12]. Therefore, it is greatly significant to research a simple, inexpensive method for preparing highly fluorescent nanoparticles with low toxicity and good photo stability.

Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials were accidentally found by researchers in the procedure of purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) fabricated by arc-discharge methods in 2004 [13]. This accidental discovery has paid great attention and provides new ideas for the fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs). Recently, FCDs have attracted greatly attentions by reason of their high fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, good photostability, low cost, and excellent biocompatibility [14–16]. They have been applied into a wide range of fields such as biosensing [17,18], sensors [19], bioimaging [20,21], photocatalysis [22] and so on. As a result, FCDs are promising to replace those highly toxic SQDs. Now, a variety of approaches for the synthesis of FCDs have been reported, such as arc discharge [23], laser ablation [24], oxidative acid treatment [25], hydrothermal synthesis [26], microwave-assisted synthesis [27], pyrolysis [14] and electrochemical etching [28].

With the development of economy, various environment problems occur and the pollution of heavy metal is harmful to human health extremely. It is acknowledge that chromium is the most common metal elements existing in the soil and the content of it is more abundant compared with other common metal elements. With the rapid development of electroplating, leather, fuel and so on, chromium and its compounds are widely used as the main materials in this field. A large amount of waste water, waste gas, and waste emissions containing chromium were discharged into the environment during the production, resulting in the environment pollution, especially the soil contamination. The pollution of chromium in the soil spreads rapidly and becomes more and more serious. Chromium is persistent in soil, better concealment and high toxicity which are poisonous to plants, animals and human health with time goes by. Some different methods have been developed for detecting metal ions, including optical methods [29], capillary electrophoresis [30] electrochemical methods [31], atomic absorption spectrometry [32], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [33] and so on, which can offer good results. However, most of these methods have some limitations, such as complicated processing, expensive instruments and time-consuming operations. Therefore, simple and inexpensive methods for the detection of metal ions are highly desirable. In addition, although FCDs have occurred as a promising probe owing to their outstanding characteristics, it has a worse selectivity for detecting heavy metal and its application is restricted. As a result, it is necessary for this work to study a method to sense for heavy metal chromium(VI) with a good selectivity. The paper prepared a new molecule based the FCDs to detect the metal ions. As a result, a lot of attention is being drawn to the research of detecting chromium and the research about it is of great significance.

Cyclodextrin (CD) is an annular polymer that composed of d-(+)-glucopyranose units molecules linked by α-(1,4) bond. α, β and γ-CD are the most commonly used, which contain six, seven, eight pyran-glucose units [34]. Among these the cyclodextrins, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) has been widely used as a dispersing reagent for insoluble chemicals and nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes, and has moderate 0.6nm cavity diameter [35,36]. Moreover, it is low production cost and currently the only mass production on industrial and application widely. β-CD has some excellent properties such as environmentally friendly, water-soluble, and can improve the solubility and stability of functional materials. In addition, due to this special cavity structure, β-CD can form stable host-guest inclusion complexes via no-covalent interactions and can remove reactive dyes, heavy metal ions and small organic molecules from aqueous solutions [37].

In this work, a fluorescent probe (FCDs-IPDI-CD) was prepared with fluorescent carbon dots, isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as raw materials; and used the probes to detect metal ions. The results show that the FCDs-IPDI-CD probe has the stronger fluorescence, high sensitivity and accuracy. The proposed method has been successfully applied for the determination of chromium ions in water and soil samples with satisfactory results. To our knowledge, this study is the first report about the preparation of FCDs-IPDI-CD and its application on the detection Cr(VI) ions in water and soil samples.

Cellulose, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI), dibutyltin dilaurate (T-12), N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), anhydrous Na2SO4 and ions solution were analytical grade and purchased from were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., in Shanghai, China. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment. The pH value of system solution was adjusted by HAc-NaAc buffer solution or NaOH solution.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum in the 4000–1000cm−1 region was recorded using a Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-IR spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Australia Varian co., LTD). UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan).

Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) were synthesized from cellulose through a simple, convenient and one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, cellulose (2.0g) and water (25mL) were put into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, which was then placed in a drying oven followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 12h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. Next, the solution had a suction filtration to exclude filter residue. And then the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 20min to dislodge the non-fluorescent deposition. Finally, the purified FCDs nanoparticles solution was obtained and stored at 4°C in the refrigerator. The fluorescent powder (FCDs) was obtained by evaporating the CDs solution for standby.

As-prepared FCDs (0.4g) were added into DMF (50mL) which were dried by anhydrous Na2SO4 and had an ultra bath for 5h at 99 frequency of sonication. Then isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI, 1.7g) and T-12 (as catalyst, 2–3 drops) were added into the above solution and the solution was reacted using water-bath methods at 80°C for 3h. After the reaction, the saturated β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) solution (50mL) and T-12 (2–3 drops) were added and the mixture was reacted using water-bath methods at 70°C for 3.5h. Next, the solution was made a suction filtration. Finally, the fluorescent probe (FCDs-IPDI-CD) powder was obtained by evaporating the above residue [34]. The synthetic route is shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD for Detection of Cr(VI) in Soil
ce:section.ce:para: At first, the soil was dried, crushed using a wooden stick and then got through the nylon sieve (2mm aperture). The sifted samples were separated by quartering again and again. Then 250g processed sample was finely grinded using an agate mortar, was made to go through the 160 mesh nylon sieve and mixed well. The work weighed 0.5g air-dried soil sample accurately (accurate to 0.0001g) and place it in the Teflon crucible. Then 5mL concentrated nitric acid was added in the sample after dampening with water and was capped. And the sample was heated at 100–150°C for 20min. After cooling down, 5mL hydrofluoric acid (HF) and 2mL perchloric acid (HClO4) were added in it and then were evaporated at about 250°C. After adding 4mL hydrochloric acid solution (1:1) and a little water in the above sample, the residue was heated to make it dissolve and was transferred to a 50mL volumetric flask. Then 10mL above solution was transferred to a 100mL volumetric flask and digestion solution of the soil was obtained. The blank solution was also prepared adopting the same method. 2mL digestion solution was transferred to a 50mL volumetric flask and then the content of chromium in soil was detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Influences of the Different Hydrothermal Temperature and Time on the Fluorescent Spectra of FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to carbonize carbohydrates through losing water in the hydrothermal process and temperature is the key factor to the formation of fluorescent carbon dots. 1g cellulose was added to 25mL distilled water and then was prepared at 160–200°C for 13h, respectively. The influence of the different hydrothermal temperatures on the fluorescent spectra is shown in Fig. 1(a). As can be seen from Fig. 1(a), initially, the fluorescence intensity increases with the increasing of temperature from 160°C to 180°C. The reason is mainly possibly that with the increase of temperature, the reaction of the FCDs is becoming more and more full; and the output of the FCDs increases, the intensity of FCDs increases. However, with increasing temperature up to 200°C, the fluorescence intensity will decrease. This is attributed to the reducing of carbon concentration, and thus the fluorescence emission reduces. However, the emission peak position is almost unchanged. Therefore, 180°C was chosen for best hydrothermal temperature in this study.

1g cellulose was added into 25mL distilled water and reacted at 180°C (above optimum conditions) for 10–14h, respectively. The influence of the different hydrothermal time on the fluorescent spectra of FCDs is shown in Fig. 1(b). As can be seen from Fig. 1(b), the fluorescence intensity enhances with the increasing of the reaction time and if the time increases unceasingly, the fluorescence intensity will decrease. The reason is mainly possibly that the reaction time is too long, the FCDs concentration decreases, and the fluorescence intensity decreases, which is similar to the theory of temperature. Therefore, the optimal experimental conditions of synthesis for FCDs were 180°C and 12h in this work.

UV–vis absorption (c) and fluorescence spectra (d) of FCDs-IPDI-CD are shown in Fig. 1. As can be seen from Fig. 1(c), FCDs-IPDI-CD solution has an absorption in the ultraviolet region and then gradually decline to the visible region where does not have any distinct absorption peak. From Fig. 1(d), the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the FCDs-IPDI-CD aqueous solution are 370 and 740nm, respectively.

TEM image of FCDs-IPDI-CD (a) and FCDs (b) are shown in Fig. 2 . As can be seen from Fig. 2(a), FCDs-IPDI-CD has a cavity form morphology structure. The dried sample could be dispersed in water and the insets of Fig. 2(a) show that the photograph of the FCDs-IPDI-CD aqueous solution (left) was yellowish color under sunlight. The photographs of the FCDs-IPDI-CD aqueous solution (right) shows an appreciable blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light. From Fig. 2(b), it clearly reveals that the product FCDs is homogeneously dispersed nanoparticles with a spherical morphology and FCDs can be freely dispersed in water.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was applied to the identification of the functional groups in the FCDs-IPDI-CD. FT-IR spectra of FCDs, β-CD and FCDs-IPDI-CD are shown in Fig. 2(c). From Fig. 2(c), the FCDs exhibit a main characteristic absorption band of OH stretching vibration mode at 3410cm−1 [38] and the peaks that ranges from 1350cm−1 to 1000cm−1 correspond to the symmetric stretching vibrations of COH and the flexural vibration of OH, respectively, which indicate that there are a large amount of the remaining hydroxyls. The characteristic peak at 3390cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration absorption bands of OH and stretching vibrations of CH2 are also detected at about 2930cm−1, which are the typical peaks of β-CD [34,37]. The obtained FCDs-IPDI-CD exhibits characteristic absorption bands of OH and CH2 stretching vibrations at 3390cm−1 and 2930cm−1, respectively; and the intensity of peak is stronger than the β-CD, which indicate that the skeleton of β-CD was not damaged. Peak around 1660cm−1 belongs to CONH vibration, indicating that the reaction between FCDs and β-CD happened and relevant characteristic absorption bands occurred. And peak at 1560cm−1 can be identified as CO stretching vibration in the carbamate groups, which indicate that the reaction between the hydroxyl (OH) of FCDs and β-CD and the NCO of IPDI was happened and the FCDs-IPDI-CD was obtained successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Effects of Some Factors on the Fluorescent Intensity of the as-Prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD Solution
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, effects of FCDs-IPDI-CD concentration, pH of solution and preservation time on the fluorescent spectra of FCDs-IPDI-CD were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . As can be seen from Fig. 3(a), with the FCDs-IPDI-CD concentration increasing from 0.01 to 0.05g/mL, the fluorescence intensity increases at first and decreases afterwards. It was clearly observed that the fluorescence intensity is dependent on the concentration of the FCDs-IPDI-CD. At the same time, the emission wavelength does not change with the increasing dosage. This may be due to decreasing interactions among the molecules at low concentrations and when the concentration is high, the phenomenon of agglomeration among the molecules may be occur [34]. As a result, 0.03g/mL of FCDs-IPDI-CD concentration was chosen as the optimum dosage in this work.

Effect of pH on the fluorescent property of FCDs-IPDI-CD was also studied at different pHs and the experiment conducted at the same excitation wavelength. The FCDs-IPDI-CD solution (0.03g/mL, pH=6.5) was diluted to different pH. As shown in Fig. 3(b), at lower pH, FCDs-IPDI-CD shows low fluorescence intensity. The FCDs-IPDI-CD system shows higher fluorescence intensity in the pH range from 6.5 to 9.5, and then the intensity decreases gradually when pH is above 9.5. The obvious quenching phenomenon occurs in the acidic and the fluorescence intensity changes little in the pH range from 6.5 to 9.5. The results may be related to the presence of the NH bonds [39,40]. The NH bonds may be protonated in acidic solutions, which will effect electronic cloud distribution of FCDs-IPDI-CD system, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. And the capacity of accepting protons or giving protons is different for the hydroxyl groups (OH) and carbamate groups (COONH) in FCDs-IPDI-CD system and hydrogen bonds can form between them. So the pH of FCDs-IPDI-CD solution has an effect on the fluorescent intensity. Therefore, 6.5–9.5 was selected as the optimum pH range in this work.

Fig. 3(c) shows the effect of the preservation time on the fluorescent intensity at the above conditions. The FCDs-IPDI-CD solution was stored for one day, 15days, 30days, 45days, 60days, 120days and 150days, respectively, which can prove the stability of as-prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD. As shown in Fig. 3(c), it is found that the FCDs-IPDI-CD still exhibits good photostability when stored at room temperature for about three months and the emission wavelength of FCDs-IPDI-CD does not shift at the same conditions and the fluorescent intensity changed little. These results reveal that the FCDs-IPDI-CD has good photostability and the influence of FCDs-IPDI-CD solution is stable, which possess great potential in the actual detecting application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Interaction Between the Fluorescent FCDs-IPDI-CD and Cr(VI) Ions
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above optimum conditions, 10mL FCDs-IPDI-CD solution and 10mL Cr(VI) (600μM, K2Cr2O7) solution were added into the colorimetric tube and mixed using the ultrasonic dispersion at 99 frequency of sonication. The fluorescence spectrum was determined on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Australia Varian co., LTD), using a quartz cuvette and the excitation and emission slit widths were 5nm (excitation wavelength, 370nm). Fluorescence spectra of FCDs-IPDI-CD in the absence and in the presence of metal ions are shown in Fig. 4 . As can be seen from Fig. 4, there is a phenomenon of Cr(VI) ions quenching of the fluorescence of FCDs-IPDI-CD. The reason may be related to some carbamate groups (COONH) and bridged bonds that can chelate with metal ions to form chelate complexes.

Effect of different concentration of Cr(VI) ions (a) on fluorescence spectra is shown in Fig. 5(a). As can be seen from Fig. 5(a), there is a phenomenon of Cr(VI) ions concentration-dependent quenching of the fluorescence intensity of FCDs-IPDI-CD at the 370nm. The fluorescence intensity of FCDs-IPDI-CD decreases gradually at the same emission peak, when the concentrations of Cr(VI) ions increases from 0μM to 600μM, indicating that this system is sensitive to Cr(VI) ions concentration.

The relative fluorescence intensity F 0/F can be obtained [40]. F0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity of FCDs-IPDI-CD at 740nm in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) ions, respectively. Plotting F 0/F against Cr(VI) ions concentration gives a straight line and the standard linear curve of the reaction system is shown in Fig. 5(b). As can be seen from Fig. 5(b), a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9976) between the relative fluorescent intensity (F 0/F) and the concentration of Cr(VI) ions is observed, showing an increased quenching over the concentration range of 0–600μM. The linear regression equation is F 0/F =0.01968C +0.96137, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 370nm in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) ions, respectively and C is the concentration of Cr(VI) ions. The detection limit is 0.56mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the Conditions for Determining Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of obtaining better detection sensitivity, the optimization conditions of detecting determining Cr(VI) such as solution pH value and action time were investigated. Effect of different pH value on relative fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 6(a). As shown in Fig. 6(a), the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of the FCDs-IPDI-CD system shows a low value under low pH conditions; and when the pH conditions are higher, the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of the FCDs-IPDI-CD system has a higher value. When the pH ranges from 3.5 to 8.5, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is not obvious and when the pH value is higher than 8.5, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon shows inconspicuous abruptly. The possible reason is that the metal ions undergo hypernomic hydrolysis. When the pH is 9.5, the fluorescence of the FCDs-IPDI-CD system before adding the Cr(VI) is stable. Moreover, when the system is under strong alkaline or strong acid conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the FCDs-IPDI-CD system is low. The relative fluorescence intensity reaches the highest at a pH value of 9.5, which is consistent with the results of the Fig. 3(b). Therefore, the pH value of 9.5 was selected in this experiment.

Effect of different reaction time on relative fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 5(b). As can be seen from Fig. 6(b), after adding the Cr(VI) solution (600μM), the dependence of the relative fluorescent intensity (F 0/F) by Cr(VI) ions reaches a stabilized value within 30min and changed little after a few hours, which indicates that the system for determining Cr(VI) ions by FCDs-IPDI-CD is stable. Therefore, 30min was selected as the optimal reaction time to determine Cr(VI) ions by FCDs-IPDI-CD.
In conclusion, the optimum conditions of detecting Cr(VI) are as follows: time is 30min and pH is 9.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Selectivity of FCDs-IPDI-CD Toward Cr(VI) Ions
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of the detection for metal ions system. In the following experiments, under the optimal detection condition (0.03g/mL of FCDs-IPDI-CD, pH=9.5 and reaction time of 30min), the selectivity detection of the common metal ions was carried out and the result is shown in Fig. 7 . The metal ions selected were Cr(VI), Fe(III), Mg(II), K(I), Fe(II) and Na(I). The concentration of different metal ions in the solution was maintained at 600μM. From Fig. 7, it is evident that the relative fluorescence intensity of system decreases remarkably after adding the Cr(VI) ions, while other mental ions (Fe(III), Mg(II), K(I), Fe(II) and Na(I)) have less impacts on the relative fluorescence intensity when compared to Cr(VI) ions [41]. The fact indicates that the obtained product has potential application on the Cr(VI) ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD for Detection of Cr(VI) in Water and Soil Samples
ce:section.ce:para: In spite of a lot of methods to detect ions, simple and effective detection of the ion still remains a challenge and the above result suggests that the as-prepared FCDs-IPDI-CDCNPs could be used as potential sensing probes under the optimal detection condition. In this work, the homemade water samples that contained Cr(VI) ions was used to study the recovery of water sample experiment by fluorescence spectrometry. In order to detect the content of Cr(VI) ions, the experiment prepared three kinds of different concentrations of water samples that contained Cr(VI) ions according to the above method. The obtained results are given in Table 1 . As can be seen from Table 1, the recovery of water sample is from 95.8% to 99.3% with satisfactory results and the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranges from 0.19% to 0.58%. The result suggests that Cr(VI) ions could be detected using FCDs-IPDI-CD as the probe in the pH=9.5 aqueous solution. Therefore, it has high sensitivity and wide linear range that the as-prepared product is used as the probe to detect Cr(VI) ions.

The proposed method was also applied to the determination of Cr(VI) in soil samples and the obtained results are given in Table 2 . From the Table 2, the recovery of detection ranges from 93.59% to 97.34% with satisfactory results. The result indicates the proposed method has been successfully applied to the detection of chromium ions in soil with satisfactory results.

In general, a novel FCDs-IPDI-CD material has been successfully prepared and used as an effective fluorescent probe for Cr(VI) ions detection. This work used the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to characterize the structure and morphology of FCDs-IPDI-CD. Effects on the fluorescent intensity of the as-prepared FCDs-IPDI-CD solution and optimization of the conditions for determining Cr(VI) were investigated. After reacting with Cr(VI) ions, the fluorescence of FCDs-IPDI-CD quenched. The influences of environment factors (such as pH, reaction time) on relative fluorescence intensity were studied. According to the optimal conditions, this work applied the FCDs-IPDI-CD as a new sensitive probe to the detection for Cr(VI) ions. And the linear dynamic curve was obtained ranged from 0 to 600μM with the correlation coefficient of 0.9976. The result indicates the proposed method has been successfully applied to the detection of Cr(VI) ions in water; and the recovery of water sample is from 95.8% to 99.3% with satisfactory results. The proposed method has also been successfully used to detect Cr(VI) ions in soil and the detection limit is 0.56mg/L; and the recovery of sample ranges from 93.59% to 97.34% with satisfactory results and the relative standard deviation was 0.49%–0.83%.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Innovation Program for Graduate Education of Jiangsu Province (KYLX15_1041), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1507115 and 31601549), the Natural Science of Jiangsu Province (BK20161362, 20160500 and 20160489) and the Natural Science of Jiangsu Education (16KJB150045 and 16KJB150009).
